comment_text: I love Charlie Sheen. I'm so glad he is making himself better. And taking responsibility for his own health and happiness. I understand what Denise is saying. My son is Bipolar and when he was first getting ill he was horrible. I had him come home to live with mum. He was single and still working but that didn't last. Ten years with the same company and his own PC business after hours. It was too much. It brought him crashing  down. He was angry and violent and loud and scared me plenty of times. I'd run outside and hide in the lane and call the Police. They'd come and find me hiding  behind a bush. They'd go into my home and talk some sense into him. And he'd settle. And I'd go back into the house. He'd come into my bedroom at 2 am and sit on my bed and cry and say sorry mum. I'd hold him and talk to him until he fell asleep. He didn't understand what was happening to him. He needed logic and order. And his mind was scattered and he was hurting. But he's brilliant now. He's a changed man now. He's 42 and living in his own place again. He's given up Alcohol and Cigarettes and is on Bipolar medication and knows more about his illness than most people. I'm very proud of him. Be proud of Charlie. He's bit the bullet. That's very hard. And he's given up his addictions. Almost impossible.  He is a Survivor, like you Denise. And he is a 🏆 🥇  Champion.  God bless you Charlie. Sending  hugs and prayers. Keep working at it EVERY single day. 💪 Love you still Charlie Sheen. 🤗💜🙏🥰

video_title: aka Charlie Sheen | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
The new Netflix documentary aka Charlie Sheen truly gripped me. I've rarely seen such an open, direct, and yet contradictory insight into the life of a Hollywood star. Charlie Sheen doesn't mince his words – and that's precisely what makes the film so captivating.

I was particularly impressed by how bluntly he speaks about his addiction: about excesses, dependence, and the destructive consequences for his life and those around him. It's rare for someone who has been so much in the spotlight to describe these depths in such detail and honesty. Some stories sound almost absurd – from wild parties to dangerous escapades – and this is precisely what makes it clear how deeply he was trapped in this spiral.

At the same time, it's not just about the shocking moments, but also about the consequences: the destroyed relationships, health consequences like his HIV diagnosis, and the difficult dynamics within his family. I was impressed by the clarity with which he identifies these things today. You sense that, on the one hand, he's still wrestling with his own past, but on the other, he's also found the strength to talk about it.

What makes the documentary so powerful is this dichotomy: Charlie remains charismatic, witty, and at times almost too casual – yet his words carry a lot of weight. This is precisely what makes his view of addiction seem authentic. He shows how seductive, destructive, and unpredictable it is without wallowing in self-pity.

aka Charlie Sheen is more than a celebrity documentary – it's an intense portrait of self-destruction, fame, and the attempt to regain control over one's own life. A film that entertains, shocks, and makes you think. Absolutely worth watching, in my opinion.♥️
